content:
# Genesis Chapter 35 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 20r–20v | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_035_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_035_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), 4th century CE — LXX | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0036 (page 32), https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0037 (page 33)

---

## Return to Bethel — The God Who Keeps His Promises

**1** Then God spoke to Jacob: "Get up. Go to Bethel and settle there. Build an altar there to the God who appeared to you when you were fleeing from your brother Esau."

**2** So Jacob said to everyone in his household — and to all who had come with him — "Get rid of the foreign gods you have with you. Purify yourselves. Put on clean clothes. **3** We are going up to Bethel, where I will build an altar to God, who answered me in my day of trouble and who has been with me everywhere I have gone."

**4** They handed over to Jacob every foreign god they had, and the rings they wore in their ears — for these were pagan amulets — and Jacob buried them all under the great oak tree at Shechem.

---

**5** Then they set out. As they traveled, a terror from God fell on all the towns around them, so that no one dared pursue Jacob's sons. **6** Jacob and all the people with him arrived at Luz — that is, Bethel — in the land of Canaan. **7** He built an altar there and called the place El-Bethel, the God of Bethel, because it was there that God had revealed himself to him when he was running away from his brother.

**8** It was there that Deborah, Rebekah's nurse, died and was buried beneath the oak below Bethel. And so it was named Allon-bacuth — the Oak of Weeping.

---

**9** God appeared to Jacob again after he came back from Paddan-aram, and blessed him. **10** God said to him:

"Your name is Jacob — but you will no longer be called Jacob. Your name is Israel."

And Israel was the name he gave him.

**11** Then God said to him:

"I am El Shaddai — God Almighty.
Be fruitful and multiply.
A nation — indeed, a great company of nations — will descend from you.
And kings will come from your own body.
**12** The land I gave to Abraham and to Isaac I now give to you.
And to your descendants after you I give this land."

**13** Then God withdrew from the place where he had spoken with him.

**14** Jacob set up a stone pillar to mark the holy place where God had spoken with him, and he poured out a drink offering on it and anointed it with oil. **15** Jacob called the place Bethel — House of God — because that was where God had spoken with him.

---

## The Death of Rachel

**16** They broke camp at Bethel and were still some distance from Ephrath when Rachel went into labor. The birth was going very badly. **17** In the worst of it, the midwife leaned over her and said, "Don't be afraid — this one is a son too." **18** But as Rachel's life was slipping away — for she was dying — she named the child Ben-oni: Son of My Sorrow. His father, however, called him Benjamin: Son of My Right Hand.

**19** Rachel died and was buried on the road to Ephrath, which is now Bethlehem. **20** Jacob set up a pillar over her grave — the same pillar that marks Rachel's tomb to this day.

---

**21** Israel moved on and set up camp beyond Migdal-eder. **22** While he was living in that region, Reuben went and slept with Bilhah, his father's concubine. And Israel heard about it.

Jacob had twelve sons:

**23** The sons of Leah: Reuben — Jacob's firstborn — then Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, and Zebulun.

**24** The sons of Rachel: Joseph and Benjamin.

**25** The sons of Bilhah, Rachel's servant: Dan and Naphtali.

**26** The sons of Zilpah, Leah's servant: Gad and Asher.

These were Jacob's sons, born to him in Paddan-aram.

---

## The Death of Isaac

**27** Jacob came home to his father Isaac at Mamre, near Kiriath-arba — that is, Hebron — where Abraham and Isaac had lived as foreigners.

**28** Isaac lived to be a hundred and eighty years old. **29** Then he breathed his last and died. He was old, full of years. And he was gathered to his people. His sons Esau and Jacob buried him.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

### The architecture of return

Genesis 35 is not simply the next episode after the violence of chapter 34. It is the deliberate counterpoint to it. Chapter 34 ended in blood and bitterness, with Jacob shamed before the Canaanites and his sons unapologetic. Chapter 35 opens with God cutting through the aftermath: *Get up. Go to Bethel.* The divine command does not address the massacre. It redirects Jacob toward the place where everything began — the place of the first vision, the ladder, the promise. God does not explain why the time to go is now. He simply calls, and the story moves.

This chapter has a three-part structure. The first movement (vv.1-15) is a return to covenant ground: the purification, the journey, the altar, the renamed patriarch, El Shaddai, and the stone pillar. The second movement (vv.16-22a) is the most emotionally devastating passage in the Jacob narrative: Rachel's death. The third movement (vv.22b-29) is an accounting — the twelve sons, and the quiet death of Isaac. The chapter moves from divine encounter to human grief to patriarchal passing. It carries the weight of endings.

---

### Verses 2-4 — The purification

**הָסִירוּ אֶת-אֱלֹהֵי הַנֵּכָר אֲשֶׁר בְּתֹכְכֶם** — "Put away the foreign gods that are among you."

This is a remarkable moment. Jacob has just come from Shechem — and in the background of this command is the incident of Genesis 31:19, where Rachel stole her father Laban's household gods (teraphim) and concealed them in her camel saddle. Whether those same idols are now what Jacob orders surrendered is not stated. But the earrings are specified as being surrendered too: **הַנְּזָמִים אֲשֶׁר בְּאָזְנֵיכֶם** — "the rings in your ears." In the ancient Near East, ear-rings could function as protective amulets associated with foreign deities. Jacob buries them all under the oak at Shechem — the same tree near which he had first entered Canaan (12:6) and at which he had already built an altar (33:20). The burying is a ritual act of severance. They go into the ground. The household crosses into new territory — literally and spiritually — without them.

PNEUMA renders "put away" rather than a more literal "remove" because the sense is one of dismissal — these gods no longer belong in Jacob's company. The action is housecleaning before a holy journey.

---

### Verse 5 — The terror of God

**וַיְהִי חִתַּת אֱלֹהִים עַל-הֶעָרִים אֲשֶׁר סְבִיבוֹתֵיהֶם** — "And the terror of God was upon the cities around them."

This phrase is the divine response to Jacob's vulnerability after the Shechem massacre. Jacob had said in 34:30: *My numbers are few — if they come against me, I will be destroyed.* The narrator now shows us the other side of that fear: the surrounding peoples are paralyzed. They do not pursue. The same God who had protected Jacob at the Jabbok now protects the whole household on the road. The word **חִתַּת** (*chittat*) — terror, dread — appears also in Exodus 23:27, where God promises to send his terror before Israel into Canaan. It is the trembling that precedes the sacred.

The Vaticanus LXX renders this as **ἔκστασις θεοῦ** — "the ecstasy [overwhelming awe] of God" — which captures the paralyzing, transcendent quality of the force that halts the surrounding nations. PNEUMA follows the Hebrew's more visceral *terror* rather than the more elevated Greek *ecstasy*, because the context is threat neutralized, not mystical rapture.

---

### Verse 7 — El-Bethel

**וַיִּקְרָא לַמָּקוֹם אֵל בֵּית-אֵל** — "And he called the place El-Bethel."

This is a compound divine name: El (God) + Bethel (House of God). The full name means something like "The God who is the House of God" or "God of the House of God." It locates the divine not just in a name but in a place — this specific ground, where the encounter happened. It is the same instinct that drives the naming throughout Genesis: place-names encode theological memory. The LXX Vaticanus simply renders this as **Βαιθηλ** (Bethel) without the El prefix, possibly flattening the compound. The Hebrew Leningrad Codex is explicit: **אֵל בֵּית-אֵל**, preserving the full divine name.

PNEUMA renders this as "El-Bethel, the God of Bethel" to preserve the double reference — both the divine title and its geographical anchor.

---

### Verse 8 — Deborah's death

This single verse stands alone, marked by the Masoretic tradition with its own paragraph division. Deborah is Rebekah's nurse — last mentioned in Genesis 24:59, who accompanied Rebekah when she left her family to marry Isaac. She has traveled with the family for decades, a living thread to the generation of Abraham and Sarah. We have not heard of her since Genesis 24. Now, without preamble or preparation, she dies under a tree below Bethel, and the tree is named *Allon-bacuth* — the Oak of Weeping.

Why does the narrator pause to record this? Deborah is a minor figure. She has no direct role in the plot. But her presence in Jacob's household is a trace of Rebekah — Jacob's mother, who sent him away to Paddan-aram and whom, as far as the text tells us, he never saw again. Deborah's death is perhaps the final closing of that connection. The name *Oak of Weeping* is not explained; it simply stands, as grief often stands — as a marked place, a name, a memorial to a mourning the text does not fully narrate but does not dismiss.

---

### Verses 10-12 — The second naming and El Shaddai

The renaming of Jacob as Israel here at Bethel (v.10) is the formal, divine confirmation of what happened at the Jabbok in chapter 32. There the wrestling angel gave Jacob a new name in the midst of combat; here at Bethel, God himself — in a direct theophany — confirms it. The two events are not doublets or contradictions. They are two moments in the same long transformation: the first is the crisis at the river; the second is the consecration at the altar.

**אֲנִי אֵל שַׁדַּי** — "I am El Shaddai." This divine name last appeared in 17:1, when God made the covenant of circumcision with Abraham. Its appearance here creates a deliberate echo: the Abrahamic promises — land, fruitfulness, kings — are now transferred in full to Jacob/Israel. The formula **פְּרֵה וּרְבֵה** ("be fruitful and multiply") echoes Genesis 1:28 and 9:1 and 17:6. Jacob stands in the line of the original human vocation and the Abrahamic promise simultaneously.

**גּוֹי וּקְהַל גּוֹיִם** — "a nation and a company of nations" — is richer than it might appear. A single people (goy) will emerge, but also a *qahal*, an assembly, a congregation of nations (goyim). The word *qahal* is later used for the assembled congregation of Israel. The promise is large enough to encompass both national identity and something that exceeds it.

El Shaddai is preserved untranslated in PNEUMA's translation notes but rendered as "God Almighty" in the text, which captures the traditional weight of the title without claiming to resolve its etymology (the root may connect to *shad*, breast/nourishment, or *shadad*, power/sufficiency — the text does not tell us).

---

### Verses 16-20 — The death of Rachel

This is the passage that defines chapter 35. Everything before it is preparation; everything after it is aftermath.

**וַתֵּלֶד וַתְּקַשׁ בְּלִדְתָּהּ** — "And she gave birth and she was in hard labor." The verb **קָשָׁה** (*qashah*) — to be hard, difficult, severe — is the same root used in Exodus 1:14 for the *harsh* labor inflicted on the Israelites in Egypt. The birth is not merely difficult; the word carries a sense of brutality, of struggle against what the body cannot bear.

The midwife's words in verse 17 — **אַל-תִּירְאִי כִּי-גַם-זֶה לָךְ בֵּן** — are among the most heartbreaking in all of Genesis. "Don't be afraid — this one is also a son." She says it as comfort; she says it to give Rachel something to hold onto in the moment of dying. The *also* is the word that carries everything: Jacob already has Joseph from Rachel. Here is a second son. The midwife is trying to give Rachel joy as she loses consciousness. The grammar of comfort in the face of death.

**בֶּן-אוֹנִי** — Ben-oni. Scholars have long debated the meaning of *oni* here. The word can mean *sorrow* (from *aven*, trouble/grief) or, in some readings, *vigor/strength* (from a related root meaning vitality). Most readings favor *sorrow*: Rachel names him with her dying breath for her pain. His father immediately renames him: **בִּנְיָמִין** — Benjamin. The name *Binyamin* is traditionally read as *Ben-yamin* — Son of the Right Hand, the south, the place of strength and honor. Jacob cannot let his son carry his mother's grief as his name. He reaches for something that faces forward.

The juxtaposition of the two names is unbearable: the mother's last act is naming her dying, and the father's first act is renaming for life. Both names are preserved in the narrative — and only one survives into history.

Rachel is buried **בְּדֶרֶךְ אֶפְרָתָה** — on the road to Ephrath/Bethlehem. Not in the family cave at Machpelah where Sarah, Abraham, Rebekah, and Isaac are buried. On the road. The woman Jacob loved most — for whom he labored fourteen years, who replaced his first seven years of labor with her father's deceit, whose very barrenness was the crisis of Genesis 30 — dies on a journey, and is buried where she falls.

Jacob sets up a **מַצֵּבָה** (*matzevah*) over her grave — the same word used for the stone pillars he has erected at holy places. He gives her grave the same mark he gives divine encounters. The text adds quietly: **הִוא מַצֶּבֶת קְבֻרַת-רָחֵל עַד-הַיּוֹם** — "That is the pillar of Rachel's tomb to this day." The narrator breaks through his own narrative into the present tense. We still know where she is buried. The grave is still there.

---

### Verse 22a — Reuben and Bilhah

**וַיֵּלֶךְ רְאוּבֵן וַיִּשְׁכַּב אֶת-בִּלְהָה פִּילֶגֶשׁ אָבִיו** — "And Reuben went and lay with Bilhah his father's concubine."

This verse arrives without preparation and without follow-up. In the Hebrew text, a paragraph break (*setumah*) separates it from what follows. The act is stated, and then — after the brief notice that "Israel heard" — the text simply moves on to the list of Jacob's sons. There is no divine response. There is no paternal explosion. There is only the silence of **וַיִּשְׁמַע יִשְׂרָאֵל** — "and Israel heard" — followed by the break.

Why did Reuben do this? The text does not explain. Some commentators suggest it was a claim to status: sleeping with the father's concubine was an assertion of authority (cf. Absalom in 2 Samuel 16:21-22). Others suggest it followed from the death of Rachel: Bilhah was Rachel's servant, and with Rachel gone, Reuben may have seen an opportunity or perceived a threat to Leah's honor. The text does not tell us. It records the act and records the silence, and that silence will echo in Genesis 49:3-4, when a dying Jacob finally says what he thought.

PNEUMA translates this with the same spare directness the Hebrew uses. Nothing is added. The text knows that adding would be wrong.

---

### Verses 22b-26 — The twelve sons

The listing of the twelve sons here — at the moment of their completion, when Benjamin has just been born — is the formal declaration that the family of Israel is assembled. The text organizes them by mother: Leah's six, Rachel's two, Bilhah's two, Zilpah's two. The list has a quiet symmetry: two mothers primary, two mothers secondary; the beloved and the unloved, the freeborn and the servant-born, all twelve names in a row. This is the nation that will become Israel.

The notation **בְּפַדַּן אֲרָם** — "in Paddan-aram" — is technically imprecise (Benjamin was just born on the road to Bethlehem, not in Paddan-aram), which some interpreters read as a textual variant. The LXX Vaticanus preserves the same notice. It may be a summary formula: these are the sons born of the Paddan-aram period, the era of Jacob's sojourning and family-building in Laban's household, now concluded. Benjamin's birth on the road marks the transition out of that era.

---

### Verses 27-29 — The death of Isaac

The chapter closes with Isaac's death, described with the language reserved for patriarchal endings: **וַיִּגְוַע יִצְחָק... שָׂבֵעַ יָמִים** — "Isaac breathed his last... full of days." The phrase *full of days* is the same used at the death of Abraham (25:8) and will be used again for Job (Job 42:17). It denotes not simply old age but completion — a life that has reached its measure, that has not been cut off or truncated.

**וַיֵּאָסֶף אֶל-עַמָּיו** — "and was gathered to his people." This is the great patriarchal idiom for death. It does not mean simply burial; it is something that happens before burial (his sons bury him afterward). It refers to a rejoining — the dead patriarch going to where the dead of his family have gone. It is the closest the Torah comes to articulating an afterlife. It does not explain what that gathering is. It simply names it, as something real, as what happens when a good man finishes his days.

Isaac's two sons — Esau and Jacob, the estranged twins — come together to bury him, just as Isaac and Ishmael once came together to bury Abraham (25:9). Grief reaches across the old divisions. The chapter that began with God commanding Jacob to return to Bethel — to the beginning of his story — ends with Jacob at his father's graveside, standing alongside the brother he had wronged long ago. The Leningrad Codex carries these last verses in the final column of folio 20v, their Hebrew letters carefully pointed, the section ending at the close of the book portion with the scribal paragraph marker before the opening of Genesis 36.

---

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 20r–20v, West Semitic Research Project (Bruce E. Zuckerman). Folio 20r (GEN_035_page_001.jpg) carries the final verses of Genesis 34 in its upper columns, then transitions at the parasha section marker into Genesis 35, continuing through approximately verse 15 across the three columns of dense Masoretic text with full vowel pointing and cantillation marks. The divine names — אֵל שַׁדַּי (El Shaddai) and אֵל בֵּית-אֵל (El-Bethel) — are clearly legible with their Masoretic pointing. Folio 20v (GEN_035_page_002.jpg) carries Genesis 35:16 through verse 29, followed by the opening verses of Genesis 36. Rachel's death narrative (vv.16-20) occupies a central section of the first column, with the names בֶּן-אוֹנִי and בִּנְיָמִין clearly visible. The paragraph break (setumah) after verse 22a's notice about Reuben and Bilhah is visible as white space before the list of sons begins. Isaac's death in verses 28-29 closes the chapter, with the patriarchal formula שָׂבֵעַ יָמִים clearly pointed. Secondary reference: Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Biblioteca Apostolica Vaticana, pages 32–33 (accessible at https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0036 and /0037). Page 32 carries the heading ΓΕΝ XXXIIII transitioning to the Genesis 35 material. Page 33 carries ΓΕΝ ΧΛ at the header and continues through the Genesis 35 narrative. Notable LXX (Vaticanus) variants: v.4 — Vaticanus includes the earrings detail as matching the MT; v.5 — Vaticanus reads ἔκστασις θεοῦ ("ecstasy of God") for MT's חִתַּת אֱלֹהִים ("terror of God"), a slightly elevated register; v.7 — Vaticanus renders El-Bethel simply as Βαιθηλ, omitting the El prefix of the MT compound divine name; v.16 — Vaticanus has ἦν χαβραθά ("it was a chabrata") for the MT's obscure distance measure kivrat-ha'arets, using a transliteration rather than translating; v.18 — Vaticanus preserves both names, Βενιαμιν and Υιος οδυνης μου ("son of my pain"), matching the MT; v.22 — Vaticanus's paragraph structure matches the MT's break after "Israel heard." DSS: No significant Genesis 35 fragments extant.*

---

*— PNEUMA*
